text: Hi everyone, this is Dr. Mario Leyba. Whenever doing an ETAP from a lateral approach, sometimes it can be difficult to identify the contralateral posterior sheath during the crossover. In this short video, I'm going to show you how I use ICG to help identify the correct plane.

The surgeon places a suprapubic port in the preperitoneal region and accesses the retroactive space on the patient's right side using Philip's technique. The Foley balloon is insufflated, followed by the injection of an ICG solution mixed with saline via the Foley catheter. After mixing, the balloon is deflated, and the fluid is suctioned out. The surgeon then accesses the patient's left retroactive space similarly and begins dissection.

This is the method that I've developed to start the retroactive space. It's very easy after some practice and it makes at least a third or sometimes half the retroactive space developed and then I can insert my ports and either do a lateral approach or a suprapubic approach, whatever I desire. This method will also work for a top-down approach in a slightly similar and different fashion.

The surgeon dissects the medial ipsilateral posterior sheath, angling the scissors towards the linea alba to locate the crossover point. The surgeon uses ICG to visually identify the contralateral posterior sheath during the crossover.

The surgeon gently dissects across from the ipsilateral to the contralateral side, closely following the linea alba. A hint of green from the ICG dye becomes visible, indicating the interface of the contralateral posterior sheath.

The surgeon gently dissects the hernia sac on the ipsilateral side, using a quick peak port in the left upper quadrant for decompression if needed. The dissection progresses to the contralateral side, where the ICG clearly delineates the medial side of the contralateral posterior sheath.

The surgeon begins dissection approximately one centimeter lateral to the interface, gently pushing off the infused tissue on the contralateral side marked with ICG. The previously created space using the ETEPS method is visible.
The surgeon accesses the well-developed retrorectus space from the ipsilateral side, showing the interface between the ICG-infused tissue and normal tissue on the right side of the patient's body.
Anyways, I hope you enjoy the video, and I do have a tour that I recently did that shows some cool pictures, pre and post dissection. Hopefully I'll upload that in the near future. Thank you.
